stem: A tree has [[v]] vertices. How many edges does it have?
solution: A tree on [[v]] vertices is connected and acyclic, so it has exactly \( [[v]]-1 = [[answer]] \) edges.
distractor_rationales: This confuses the vertex count with the edge count, as if every vertex contributed its own edge.
This overshoots the correct edge count by 2 relative to the tree property.
This uses the complete-graph edge formula instead of the tree edge formula.
This mistakenly halves the vertex count instead of subtracting 1.
This overshoots the correct edge count by 2.
This undershoots the correct edge count by 1.

stem: A forest (a graph with no cycles) has [[v]] vertices spread across exactly [[c]] connected components, each of which is itself a tree. How many edges does the forest have in total?
solution: Each of the [[c]] tree components with a total of [[v]] vertices contributes (component size \(-1\)) edges, so summing over components gives \( [[v]] - [[c]] = [[answer]] \) edges total.
distractor_rationales: This overshoots the correct total by 1.
This undershoots the correct total by 1.
This divides the vertex count by the number of components instead of subtracting.
This uses the single-tree formula, ignoring that there are [[c]] separate components.
This adds an unrelated adjustment of [[c]] to the correct total.
This subtracts an unrelated adjustment of [[c]] from the correct total.

stem: A graph with [[v]] vertices currently has exactly [[c]] connected components. What is the MINIMUM number of additional edges that must be added to make the graph connected (a single component)?
solution: Each new edge can merge at most two components into one, so reducing [[c]] components down to 1 requires at least \( [[c]]-1 = [[answer]] \) edges.
distractor_rationales: This forgets that connecting the LAST pair of components requires one fewer edge than the total component count.
This overshoots the correct minimum by 1.
This confuses the edge count of the resulting spanning forest with the number of NEW edges needed.
This divides the vertex count by the component count instead of subtracting 1 from the component count.
This overshoots the correct minimum by 1.
This undershoots the correct minimum by 1, allowing too few edges.

stem: A connected graph has [[v]] vertices and [[e]] edges. What is the minimum number of edges that must be removed so that the remaining graph is still connected but has no cycles (i.e. is a spanning tree)?
solution: A spanning tree on [[v]] vertices needs exactly \( [[v]]-1 \) edges, so the number of excess (cycle-creating) edges that must be removed is \( [[e]] - ([[v]]-1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by one from the correct excess edge count.
This subtracts in the wrong direction, giving a negative or nonsensical count.
This divides the edge count by the vertex count instead of subtracting the spanning-tree size.
This reports the required SPANNING TREE size rather than the number of edges to remove.
This overshoots the correct excess edge count by 1.
This undershoots the correct excess edge count by 1.

stem: A forest (a graph with no cycles) has [[v]] vertices and [[e]] edges. How many connected components does it have?
solution: Each edge in a forest reduces the number of components by exactly one from the starting count of [[v]] isolated vertices, so the number of components is \( [[v]] - [[e]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This subtracts in the wrong direction, giving a negative or nonsensical count.
This overshoots the correct component count by 1.
This undershoots the correct component count by 1.
This divides the vertex count by the edge count instead of subtracting.
This overshoots the correct component count by 1.
This undershoots the correct component count by 1.
